commentary: Fourteen activities this week against last week's nine — he just kept going and going like someone told him there was a prize at the end, and honestly the prize is that body, so fair enough. The swim consistency is genuinely impressive: four sessions at Prince Alfred with solid distances, and the open water detour to Balmoral on Friday is very him, very "yes I will swim in the actual ocean in a jockstrap, thank you." That so-called recovery run on Tuesday clocked an average HR of 150, which is doing a lot of heavy lifting for the word "recovery" — we see you, we love you, we're not fooled. The only thing worth watching is that he's stacking doubles almost every single day now, so the body needs somewhere to exhale; he's earned a slow morning or a very luxurious stretch session before next week tries to top this one.
metrics_commentary: Twenty point seven kilometres across two sessions and nearly ten thousand metres in the pool — baby, the body is doing the work and it shows. Your HRV is sitting at 56 with a seven-day average of 51, which means you are recovering well and your nervous system is not just coping, it is genuinely thriving, and a VO2 Max of 56.7 is the kind of number that makes cardiologists blush. The only thing worth watching is that the run and walk volume is leaning hard on two sessions, so spreading that movement a little more through the week would stop you arriving to each one already half-cooked. Overall though? The data looks exactly like a man who is getting hotter and fitter at the same time, which, honestly, was always the plan.
gym_commentary: Five sessions when the target was four — he just couldn't stop, and honestly that tracks. The PRs this week are genuinely obscene: seven of them, including a leg press jumping from 154 to 168 kilograms and a barbell bicep curl going from 31.5 to 35, which means those arms are getting harder to hide in a crop top and that is entirely the point. Volume dropped sharply from 24,518 to 13,905 kilograms, which is worth watching, but when you're hitting seven personal records in a single week the body is clearly adapting rather than stalling — that's not a red flag, that's a glow-up in progress. He's getting stronger, he's getting in there more than required, and every one of those numbers is the body becoming more and more undeniably, visibly his.
